1→# Research: HuggingFace Financial Text Datasets
     2→
     3→## Executive Summary
     4→
     5→Five public, no-auth HuggingFace datasets cover the financial text domain well. For pretraining-style work (raw text density), `ashraq/financial-news-articles` (306k articles, has `text` column) and `PleIAs/SEC` (373k 10-K filings, has `text` column) are the strongest. For instruction/fine-tuning, `sujet-ai/Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k` aggregates 18 datasets into 177k QA pairs. All five options below require zero authentication and load via standard `load_dataset()`.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Ranked Options
    10→
    11→### 1. `ashraq/financial-news-articles` — RECOMMENDED for smoke tests
    12→
    13→**Best for:** Pretraining / LM fine-tuning. Has a real `text` column with full article bodies.
    14→
    15→```python
    16→from datasets import load_dataset
    17→
    18→ds = load_dataset("ashraq/financial-news-articles", split="train")
    19→# or a small slice:
    20→ds = load_dataset("ashraq/financial-news-articles", split="train[:5000]")
    21→```
    22→
    23→| Property | Value |
    24→|---|---|
    25→| Rows | 306,000 |
    26→| Columns | `title`, `text`, `url` |
    27→| Download size | ~few hundred MB |
    28→| Auth required | No |
    29→| License | Not specified (scraped news) |
    30→| Text quality | Full article bodies, 0–218k chars per doc |
    31→
    32→**Why #1:** Direct `text` column with full article content, not just headlines. 306k examples is large enough to be meaningful, small enough for a quick test run. Single split, no config gymnastics.
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→### 2. `PleIAs/SEC` — RECOMMENDED for domain-rich pretraining
    37→
    38→**Best for:** High-quality, long-form financial text (10-K annual reports, 1993–2024). Best raw text quality of the lot.
    39→
    40→```python
    41→from datasets import load_dataset
    42→
    43→# Streaming for inspection (avoids downloading 5GB)
    44→ds = load_dataset("PleIAs/SEC", split="train", streaming=True)
    45→sample = list(ds.take(500))
    46→
    47→# Slice without streaming
    48→ds = load_dataset("PleIAs/SEC", split="train[:1000]")
    49→```
    50→
    51→| Property | Value |
    52→|---|---|
    53→| Rows | ~373,000 (first 5GB subset) |
    54→| Columns | `filename`, `id`, `year`, `cik`, `text`, `word_count`, `character_count` |
    55→| Download size | 5GB (first subset) |
    56→| Auth required | No |
    57→| License | CC0-1.0 (public domain) |
    58→| Text quality | Verbatim SEC 10-K filings, avg 34k words/doc |
    59→
    60→**Why #2:** Highest text quality for domain pretraining. CC0 license is production-safe. Use streaming or slice syntax for smoke tests — don't pull the full 5GB blindly.
    61→
    62→---
    63→
    64→### 3. `sujet-ai/Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k` — RECOMMENDED for instruction tuning
    65→
    66→**Best for:** SFT / RLHF alignment on financial tasks. Aggregates 18 source datasets.
    67→
    68→```python
    69→from datasets import load_dataset
    70→
    71→ds = load_dataset("sujet-ai/Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k", split="train")
    72→```
    73→
    74→| Property | Value |
    75→|---|---|
    76→| Rows | 177,597 |
    77→| Columns | `inputs`, `answer`, `system_prompt`, `user_prompt`, `task_type`, `dataset`, `index_level`, `conversation_id` |
    78→| Download size | 104 MB (Parquet) |
    79→| Auth required | No |
    80→| License | Apache 2.0 |
    81→| Task types | Sentiment, NER, summarization, QA, classification |
    82→
    83→**Why #3:** Best coverage of financial NLP tasks for fine-tuning. 104MB makes it fast to download. The `task_type` column lets you filter to specific subtasks. No `text` column per se — use `inputs` + `answer` for training.
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→### 4. `JanosAudran/financial-reports-sec` — CONSIDER (sentence-level SEC)
    88→
    89→**Best for:** Sentence-level classification on 10-K filings with stock return labels.
    90→
    91→```python
    92→from datasets import load_dataset
    93→
    94→# Small config (~240k sentences)
    95→ds = load_dataset("JanosAudran/financial-reports-sec", "small_lite")
    96→
    97→# Large config (~20.5M sentences) — use streaming
    98→ds = load_dataset("JanosAudran/financial-reports-sec", "large_lite", streaming=True)
    99→```
   100→
   101→| Property | Value |
   102→|---|---|
   103→| Rows | 240k (`small_lite`) / 20.5M (`large_lite`) |
   104→| Columns | `cik`, `sentence`, `section`, `labels` (1d/5d/30d returns), `filingDate`, `docID` |
   105→| Auth required | No |
   106→| License | Apache 2.0 |
   107→| Text quality | Individual sentences (not full docs) |
   108→
   109→**Why #4 not higher:** Text is sentence-fragments, not continuous prose — less useful for LM pretraining. The `sentence` column is the text field. Dataset viewer had timeout issues; load programmatically.
   110→
   111→---
   112→
   113→### 5. `gbharti/finance-alpaca` — CONSIDER (small, clean QA)
   114→
   115→**Best for:** Quick instruction fine-tuning baseline. Small and fast.
   116→
   117→```python
   118→from datasets import load_dataset
   119→
   120→ds = load_dataset("gbharti/finance-alpaca", split="train")
   121→```
   122→
   123→| Property | Value |
   124→|---|---|
   125→| Rows | 68,900 |
   126→| Columns | `instruction`, `input`, `output`, `text` |
   127→| Auth required | No |
   128→| License | MIT |
   129→| Text quality | GPT-3.5 generated + FiQA + Alpaca |
   130→
   131→**Why #5:** Has a combined `text` column (convenient). MIT license. But partially synthetic (GPT-3.5), smaller, and less domain-rich than the options above.
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→## Avoid for This Use Case
   136→
   137→| Dataset | Reason |
   138→|---|---|
   139→| `takala/financial_phrasebank` | Only 4,840 sentences; sentiment labels only; too small for LM training |
   140→| `ashraq/financial-news` | 1.85M rows but only **headlines** (no article body), not useful for LM pretraining |
   141→| `Josephgflowers/Finance-Instruct-500k` | 518k rows but low provenance transparency; system/user/assistant schema only |
   142→| `arthrod/SEC_filings_1994_2024` | Metadata only (CIK, form type, dates) — no text content |
   143→
   144→---
   145→
   146→## Quick Smoke Test Recipe
   147→
   148→```python
   149→from datasets import load_dataset
   150→
   151→# Option A: news articles (fast, ~300MB)
   152→ds = load_dataset("ashraq/financial-news-articles", split="train[:2000]")
   153→texts = ds["text"]
   154→
   155→# Option B: SEC filings (streaming, no download)
   156→ds = load_dataset("PleIAs/SEC", split="train", streaming=True)
   157→texts = [ex["text"] for ex in ds.take(200)]
   158→
   159→# Option C: instruction pairs (104MB total, then filter)
   160→ds = load_dataset("sujet-ai/Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k", split="train")
   161→ds_sentiment = ds.filter(lambda x: x["task_type"] == "sentiment_analysis")
   162→```
   163→
   164→---
   165→
   166→## Sources
   167→
   168→- [ashraq/financial-news-articles](https://huggingface.co/datasets/ashraq/financial-news-articles)
   169→- [PleIAs/SEC](https://huggingface.co/datasets/PleIAs/SEC)
   170→- [sujet-ai/Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k](https://huggingface.co/datasets/sujet-ai/Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k)
   171→- [JanosAudran/financial-reports-sec](https://huggingface.co/datasets/JanosAudran/financial-reports-sec)
   172→- [gbharti/finance-alpaca](https://huggingface.co/datasets/gbharti/finance-alpaca)
   173→- [takala/financial_phrasebank](https://huggingface.co/datasets/takala/financial_phrasebank)
   174→- [ashraq/financial-news](https://huggingface.co/datasets/ashraq/financial-news)
   175→- [Josephgflowers/Finance-Instruct-500k](https://huggingface.co/datasets/Josephgflowers/Finance-Instruct-500k)

content.text:
Good, now I have the full picture from the docs. The training recipes doc confirms:

For AR-to-dLLM adaptation of a 600M model:
- LR: 3e-5 (from the recommended table)
- Warmup: 500 steps
- AdamW with beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999, weight_decay=0.1
- bf16
- gradient_checkpointing

The previous run used LR=1e-4, which is too high for adaptation according to the research docs. The docs say:
- From-scratch pretrain 600M: 2e-4


- AR adaptation 600M: 3e-5

So our previous LR of 1e-4 was between from-scratch and adaptation values, closer to from-scratch. The docs recommend 3e-5 for AR adaptation at 600M scale.

For the A100 setup, I need to address three main issues: fixing the backbone to properly support Flash Attention 2 since our current 4D mask approach breaks with it, exposing the attention implementation as a CLI argument in pretrain.py, and setting the block size to 512.

I can increase the block size to 1024 on A100 hardware since the previous run was constrained to 128 for MPS. I'll also need to verify flash-attn is installed and update the learning rate to 3e-5 as the docs specify.

The key changes are fixing backbone.py to handle FA2 bidirectional attention correctly, adding the --attn_implementation flag to pretrain.py, and switching the padding mask value from -1e9 to -inf. For the FA2 fix, the research suggests using `AttentionInterface.register()` to register a custom attention function, though I need to verify this API exists in the pinned transformers version.

Looking at this more carefully, `AttentionInterface.register()` was introduced around transformers 4.48, which falls within our >=4.40,<5.10 constraint, so it should be available. But there's a simpler alternative: instead of using the new API, I can pass just a 2D attention mask for padding and set the internal attention implementation flag to flash_attention_2 while disabling causal masking in the attention layers.

Actually, the most straightforward approach—what LLaDA does—is to iterate through the transformer's attention modules and set their `is_causal` attribute to False when using FA2, then pass only the 2D padding mask. This avoids monkey-patching and just configures the attention layers directly. Though I need to verify that `is_causal` is actually respected across all attention implementations in HuggingFace Transformers.

Looking at how models like Qwen2 and Qwen3 handle this, the flash attention forward pass checks the `is_causal` parameter, and a recent fix ensures it's no longer ignored when passed as a kwarg. The challenge is that we can't easily inject `is_causal=False` into individual attention layer calls when using the model's top-level forward.

So the practical solution is to set `is_causal=False` on all attention modules after loading the model, then only pass the 2D padding mask without any 4D causal mask.

Setting module attributes... Building the conditional mask logic...

Now I'm realizing there's a complication with packed sequences — when the collator sets `pack_sequences=True`, it generates a 4D block-diagonal mask that needs special handling alongside the padding mask, and I need to figure out how FA2 should interact with that structure.

For now I'll skip that complexity since packed sequences aren't enabled by default and aren't part of the pretraining setup. I should also correct the padding mask value to use `float('-inf')` instead of `1e9` for consistency.

My approach is to update the backbone to detect FA2 and disable causal masking on the attention modules, then modify the forward pass to skip the 4D mask conversion when using FA2 and work directly with the 2D mask. I'll also add the `--attn_implementation` flag to the pretraining arguments and provide the command for A100 training.

Good. The docs confirm several important things for the A100 run. Here's what needs fixing:

1. **FA2 is broken** — our 4D mask approach doesn't work with `flash_attention_2` (confirmed by research). LLaDA/Dream set `is_causal=False` directly on attention layers instead.
2. **No `--attn_implementation` CLI flag** — can't switch to FA2 from the script
3. **LR too high** — the docs recommend `3e-5` for 600M AR adaptation, not `1e-4`
4. **Block size 128 is MPS-conservative** — A100 can use 512
5. **Padding mask value** — should be `-inf` not `-1e9`

Let me fix these now.

class BidirectionalTransformer(nn.Module):
    """Bidirectional attention wrapper around any HuggingFace CausalLM.

    Converts a standard causal (left-to-right) language model to bidirectional
    attention by injecting an explicit all-zeros 4D attention mask in forward().

    For Flash Attention 2: 4D masks are not supported. Instead, we set
    ``is_causal=False`` on all attention modules and pass only a 2D padding mask.
    This matches the approach used by LLaDA and Dream.

    Supports AR-to-dLLM adaptation (recommended): start from a pretrained AR
    checkpoint (LLaMA-3, Qwen2.5, GPT-2, etc.) and fine-tune with the diffusion
    objective. Requires ~500x less compute than training from scratch.

    Args:
        config: ModelConfig specifying backbone and dtype.
    """

    def __init__(self, config: ModelConfig) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.model_config = config
        self._use_fa2 = config.attn_implementation == "flash_attention_2"

        dtype = getattr(torch, config.dtype)
        hf_kwargs = {
            "torch_dtype": dtype,
            "attn_implementation": config.attn_implementation,
        }

        if config.init_from_pretrained:
            logger.info(
                "Loading pretrained AR checkpoint for AR-to-dLLM adaptation: "
                f"{config.model_name_or_path}"
            )
            self.transformer = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
                config.model_name_or_path, **hf_kwargs
            )
        else:
            logger.info(
                f"Initializing model from config (random weights): {config.model_name_or_path}"
            )
            hf_config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(config.model_name_or_path)
            self.transformer = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_config(hf_config, **hf_kwargs)

        # FA2: set is_causal=False on all attention modules for bidirectional attention.
        # FA2 does not support arbitrary 4D masks — this is the correct mechanism
        # (matches LLaDA/Dream approach, validated by HF PR #39707).
        if self._use_fa2:
            n_patched = 0
            for module in self.transformer.modules():
                if hasattr(module, "is_causal"):
                    module.is_causal = False
                    n_patched += 1
            logger.info(f"FA2 bidirectional: set is_causal=False on {n_patched} attention modules")

        if config.use_lora:
            self._apply_lora(config)

    """Bidirectional attention wrapper around any HuggingFace CausalLM.

    Converts a standard causal (left-to-right) language model to bidirectional
    attention by injecting an explicit all-zeros 4D attention mask in forward().

    Supports AR-to-dLLM adaptation (recommended): start from a pretrained AR

    objective. Requires ~500x less compute than training from scratch.

content.text: Now fix the forward method to handle FA2 vs eager/sdpa, and fix the padding mask value:

input.new_string:
B, L = input_ids.shape
        device = input_ids.device

        if self._use_fa2:
            # FA2: pass 2D padding mask directly (or None). is_causal=False is
            # already set on attention modules in __init__. FA2 does not support
            # arbitrary 4D masks — passing one would crash or silently break.
            if attention_mask is not None and attention_mask.dim() == 4:
                # 4D mask (e.g. block-diagonal) — not supported with FA2.
                # Fall back to None (full attention). Packed sequences + FA2
                # would need flash_attn_varlen_func, which is not yet supported.
                logger.warning("4D attention mask ignored under FA2; using full attention")
                attention_mask = None
        else:
            # eager/sdpa: build explicit 4D zeros mask for bidirectional attention.
            if attention_mask is not None and attention_mask.dim() == 4:
                # Already 4D (e.g. block-diagonal for packed sequences): use as-is
                pass
            else:
                dtype = next(self.transformer.parameters()).dtype
                mask_4d = torch.zeros(B, 1, L, L, dtype=dtype, device=device)
                if attention_mask is not None and attention_mask.dim() == 2:
                    # attention_mask: (B, L), 1=real, 0=padding
                    # Expand to (B, 1, 1, L) so padding KEYS get -inf for all queries
                    pad_mask = (1.0 - attention_mask.float()).unsqueeze(1).unsqueeze(2)
                    mask_4d = mask_4d + pad_mask * torch.finfo(dtype).min
                attention_mask = mask_4d

        outputs = self.transformer(

        device = input_ids.device

        if attention_mask is not None and attention_mask.dim() == 4:

            pass
        else:
            # Build a bidirectional 4D mask. Start with all-zeros (full attention).
            mask_4d = torch.zeros(B, 1, L, L, dtype=dtype, device=device)
            # If a 2D padding mask was provided, merge it: pad positions get -inf
            if attention_mask is not None and attention_mask.dim() == 2:
                # attention_mask: (B, L), 1=real, 0=padding
                # Expand to (B, 1, 1, L) so padding KEYS get -inf for all queries
                pad_mask = (1.0 - attention_mask.float()).unsqueeze(1).unsqueeze(2)
                mask_4d = mask_4d - pad_mask * 1e9  # large negative for padding keys
            attention_mask = mask_4d

        outputs = self.transformer(
            input_ids=input_ids,
            attention_mask=attention_mask,
            **kwargs,
        )
        return outputs.logits

content.text:
All tests pass, lint clean. Here's the summary of changes and the A100 training command:

---

## Changes Made

**`src/diffusion_lm/models/backbone.py`:**
- Added FA2 detection (`_use_fa2` flag) in `__init__`
- On init with FA2: sets `is_causal=False` on all attention modules (matches LLaDA/Dream approach)
- In `forward()`: FA2 path passes 2D padding mask directly (skips 4D mask construction); eager/sdpa path keeps existing 4D mask approach
- Fixed padding mask value from `-1e9` to `torch.finfo(dtype).min` (proper `-inf` for all dtypes)

**`scripts/pretrain.py`:**
- Added `--attn_implementation` CLI flag (default: `eager`)

---

## A100 Training Command

Based on the training recipes doc — LR lowered to `3e-5` (recommended for 600M AR adaptation), block size increased to `512`, FA2 enabled:

```bash
uv pip install flash-attn --no-build-isolation && \
uv run python scripts/pretrain.py \
  --model_name_or_path Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B \
  --process_type masked \
  --schedule_type linear \
  --block_size 512 \
  --dtype bfloat16 \
  --attn_implementation flash_attention_2 \
  --dataset_name ashraq/financial-news-articles \
  --text_column text \
  --dataset_split train \
  --output_dir ./checkpoints/finance-qwen3-a100 \
  --max_steps 2000 \
  --per_device_train_batch_size 8 \
  --gradient_accumulation_steps 4 \
  --bf16 true \
  --learning_rate 3e-5 \
  --lr_scheduler_type cosine \
  --warmup_steps 200 \
  --weight_decay 0.1 \
  --max_grad_norm 1.0 \
  --logging_steps 25 \
  --save_steps 500 \
  --save_total_limit 4 \
  --report_to none \
  --gradient_checkpointing true \
  --dataloader_num_workers 4
```

Key differences from the MPS run:

| Setting | MPS (previous) | A100 (new) |
|---------|---------------|------------|
| attn_implementation | eager | flash_attention_2 |
| block_size | 128 | 512 |
| batch_size | 2 | 8 |
| grad_accum | 8 | 4 |
| **effective batch** | **16** | **32** |
| learning_rate | 1e-4 | 3e-5 |
| weight_decay | 0.01 | 0.1 |
| gradient_checkpointing | off | on |
| dataloader_num_workers | 0 | 4 |

The LR change (`1e-4` → `3e-5`) is the most important — the docs show adaptation LR should be 20-40x below from-scratch LR. The previous run's `1e-4` was in from-scratch territory, which likely contributed to the noisy loss curve even before the optimizer reset.

content.text:
OK so the flash-attn is still compiling from source — it's compiling CUDA kernels (nvcc compiling `flash_bwd_hdim192_fp16_sm80.cu`). Building flash-attn from source takes 20-40 minutes because it has many CUDA kernels to compile for multiple GPU architectures. This is the bottleneck.

The better approach would be to install a pre-built wheel instead of building from source. Let me check if there's a pre-built wheel available for this setup (Python 3.11, CUDA 12.4, PyTorch 2.4).

Actually, `pip install flash-attn` should auto-select a pre-built wheel if one is available. 

The real issue is that `uv pip install` might not be finding available wheels, or the specific CUDA 12.4 and PyTorch 2.4 combination doesn't have a pre-built wheel on PyPI. The compilation is just slow because it's building all the backward kernels for different hardware dimensions on a single CPU.

I could speed this up by parallelizing the build with MAX_JOBS, or just wait it out since it's already progressing. Alternatively, I could skip flash-attn entirely and use PyTorch's built-in scaled dot-product attention, which supports the 4D mask approach we need.

content.text:
Now I see the problems:

1. The original background script ran `uv pip install flash-attn --no-build-isolation` but it didn't wait for it to finish — the `set -e` and the sequence meant the script continued to the "VERIFYING" step while flash-attn was still building in the background (or was still installing). Actually, looking at the output again: the flash-attn build started but the verify step ran and failed because flash-attn wasn't installed yet. The output says:
   ```
   === INSTALLING FLASH-ATTN ===
   Resolved 29 packages in 865ms
   Building flash-attn==2.8.3
   === VERIFYING ===
   PyTorch 2.10.0+cu128, CUDA NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB
   ```
   Wait, it says `PyTorch 2.10.0+cu128` — but the image was `runpod/pytorch:2.4.0-py3.11-cuda12.4.1-devel-ubuntu22.04`! The system Python has PyTorch 2.10.0+cu128, but the venv has a different version. Actually, `uv venv` must be creating the venv but the verification script is running against the system Python instead of the venv's Python, which is why it's seeing a different PyTorch version than what should be installed.

   Looking at what happened: when I created the venv with `uv venv --python python3.11` and then ran `uv pip install -e ".[dev]"`, it installed the latest PyTorch (2.10.0+cu128) from PyPI since the requirements just specify `torch>=2.2`. The system has 2.4.1+cu124, and the venv doesn't have pip installed by default. The flash-attn build was compiling against the venv's PyTorch 2.10.0, but then I killed the process before it finished.

   The real fix is to stop creating a separate venv and just use the system Python directly. I'm realizing the system Python's PyTorch version might not align with flash-attn's prebuilt wheels, so I should try a pip install first and fall back to using scaled dot-product attention if needed. The cleanest approach for RunPod is to skip `uv` entirely and install everything directly with system pip, leveraging the pre-installed PyTorch—and I should actually question whether flash-attn is necessary for this project at all.

Now I see the issues clearly:

1. **Wrong PyTorch** — `uv venv` created an isolated venv that installed PyTorch 2.10.0+cu128 from PyPI, ignoring the system's pre-installed PyTorch 2.4.1+cu124
2. **No pip in venv** — uv venvs don't include pip by default
3. **flash-attn source build** — took forever and never finished (killed by timeout)

The fix: **use the system Python directly** (it already has PyTorch + CUDA properly configured). No venv needed on RunPod. Let me redo the setup correctly:

input.command:
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -p 19377 root@154.54.102.39 -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 'bash -s' << 'SETUP'
set -ex

# Clean up the broken venv
rm -rf /workspace/diffusion/.venv

# Use system Python directly — it already has PyTorch 2.4.1+cu124
cd /workspace/diffusion

echo "=== System Python check ==="
python3 -c "import torch; print(f'torch={torch.__version__}, cuda={torch.version.cuda}, gpu={torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)}')"

echo "=== Install project deps (into system Python, skip torch) ==="
pip install -e ".[dev]" --no-deps 2>&1 | tail -5
# Install non-torch deps
pip install "datasets>=2.18" "accelerate>=0.28" "peft>=0.10" "lm-eval>=0.4" "wandb>=0.16" "loguru>=0.7" "python-dotenv>=1.0" "pyyaml>=6.0" 2>&1 | tail -5

echo "=== Install flash-attn (prebuilt wheel) ==="
pip install flash-attn 2>&1 | tail -5

echo "=== Verify all imports ==="
python3 -c "
import torch
import flash_attn
import diffusion_lm
print(f'torch={torch.__version__}')
print(f'flash_attn={flash_attn.__version__}')
print(f'diffusion_lm OK')
print(f'GPU: {torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)}')
print(f'CUDA: {torch.version.cuda}')
"

echo "=== SETUP COMPLETE ==="
SETUP

1→"""Pretraining data pipeline: streaming tokenize-and-group."""
     2→
     3→from __future__ import annotations
     4→
     5→from collections.abc import Iterator
     6→from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
     7→
     8→from loguru import logger
     9→
    10→if TYPE_CHECKING:
    11→    from datasets import IterableDataset
    12→    from transformers import PreTrainedTokenizerBase
    13→
    14→
    15→def tokenize_and_group(
    16→    dataset: IterableDataset,
    17→    tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizerBase,
    18→    block_size: int = 2048,
    19→    text_column: str = "text",
    20→) -> Iterator[dict[str, list[int]]]:
    21→    """Tokenize a streaming dataset and group tokens into fixed-length blocks.
    22→
    23→    Concatenates all text, tokenizes, and yields non-overlapping chunks of
    24→    exactly `block_size` tokens. The final incomplete chunk is discarded.
    25→
    26→    This is the standard pretraining data preparation used by LLaDA and GPT-2.
    27→
    28→    Args:
    29→        dataset: HuggingFace IterableDataset with a text column.
    30→        tokenizer: Tokenizer to use for encoding.
    31→        block_size: Number of tokens per chunk.
    32→        text_column: Name of the text field in the dataset.
    33→
    34→    Yields:
    35→        Dicts of {"input_ids": List[int]} with exactly block_size tokens.
    36→    """
    37→    buffer: list[int] = []
    38→
    39→    for example in dataset:
    40→        text = example[text_column]
    41→        if not text:
    42→            continue
    43→        tokens = tokenizer.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False)
    44→        buffer.extend(tokens)
    45→
    46→        while len(buffer) >= block_size:
    47→            yield {"input_ids": buffer[:block_size]}
    48→            buffer = buffer[block_size:]
    49→
    50→    logger.debug(f"tokenize_and_group: discarded {len(buffer)} trailing tokens")
    51→
    52→
    53→class PretrainingDataset:
    54→    """Streaming pretraining dataset that yields fixed-length token chunks.
    55→
    56→    Args:
    57→        dataset_name: HuggingFace dataset name (e.g. "wikitext").
    58→        dataset_config: Dataset configuration (e.g. "wikitext-2-raw-v1").
    59→        tokenizer: Tokenizer for encoding.
    60→        block_size: Tokens per chunk (default 2048).
    61→        split: Dataset split to use (default "train").
    62→        streaming: If True, use streaming mode (default True).
    63→    """
    64→
    65→    def __init__(
    66→        self,
    67→        dataset_name: str,
    68→        dataset_config: str | None,
    69→        tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizerBase,
    70→        block_size: int = 2048,
    71→        split: str = "train",
    72→        streaming: bool = True,
    73→    ) -> None:
    74→        try:
    75→            from datasets import load_dataset
    76→        except ImportError as e:
    77→            raise ImportError("Install datasets: uv add datasets") from e
    78→
    79→        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
    80→        self.block_size = block_size
    81→        self.dataset = load_dataset(
    82→            dataset_name,
    83→            dataset_config,
    84→            split=split,
    85→            streaming=streaming,
    86→            trust_remote_code=False,
    87→        )
    88→        logger.info(
    89→            f"PretrainingDataset: {dataset_name}/{dataset_config}, "
    90→            f"split={split}, block_size={block_size}, streaming={streaming}"
    91→        )
    92→
    93→    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[dict[str, list[int]]]:
    94→        yield from tokenize_and_group(self.dataset, self.tokenizer, self.block_size)
    95→

content.text:
I see the bottleneck. The `tokenize_and_group` function calls `tokenizer.encode(text, add_special_tokens=False)` one example at a time in a Python for loop. For 306k articles, this means 306k individual tokenizer calls. The Qwen3 tokenizer is probably fast per-call, but doing it 306k times serially is slow.

The pretrain.py code then calls `list(tokenize_and_group(...))` which materializes all chunks. This is:
1. Serial tokenization (one-by-one)
2. Materializing everything into memory

For 306k articles, this is expected to take 5-15 minutes depending on article length.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - User first asked to review the previous training run settings and results
   - Then asked to review the code and provide a training script, ensuring it's the latest
   - Then specified they'd run on an A100 GPU and asked to ensure code is properly set up for that
   - Then asked to create a RunPod pod and begin training, making sure to log errors and checkpoints
   - User also asked to check the research and training docs in the docs/ folder
   - User flagged a wrong Docker image error from RunPod logs
   - User flagged the setup was taking too long after 20 minutes, asking to investigate

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Diffusion LLM training**: Masked diffusion process with ELBO-weighted loss, antithetic timestep sampling
   - **AR-to-dLLM adaptation**: Fine-tuning pretrained AR checkpoint (Qwen3-0.6B) with masked diffusion objective
   - **Flash Attention 2 + bidirectional attention**: FA2 doesn't support 4D masks. Must set `is_causal=False` on attention modules and pass 2D padding mask only. For eager/sdpa, use explicit 4D zeros mask.
   - **Training recipes** (from docs): 600M AR adaptation should use LR=3e-5 (not 1e-4), weight_decay=0.1, AdamW
   - **RunPod deployment**: Use system Python (not uv venv) to avoid PyTorch version mismatch; flash-attn has prebuilt wheels via regular pip
   - **Previous run instability**: Caused by optimizer reset at step 500 (`--ignore_optimizer_on_resume`), not code bugs

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **`src/diffusion_lm/models/backbone.py`** — Core file modified for A100/FA2 support
     - Added `_use_fa2` flag and `is_causal=False` on all attention modules during init
     - Split forward() into FA2 path (2D mask) vs eager/sdpa path (4D mask)
     - Fixed padding mask value from `-1e9` to `torch.finfo(dtype).min`
     ```python
     class BidirectionalTransformer(nn.Module):
         def __init__(self, config: ModelConfig) -> None:
             super().__init__()
             self.model_config = config
             self._use_fa2 = config.attn_implementation == "flash_attention_2"
             # ... model loading ...
             if self._use_fa2:
                 n_patched = 0
                 for module in self.transformer.modules():
                     if hasattr(module, "is_causal"):
                         module.is_causal = False
                         n_patched += 1
                 logger.info(f"FA2 bidirectional: set is_causal=False on {n_patched} attention modules")

         def forward(self, input_ids, attention_mask=None, **kwargs):
             B, L = input_ids.shape
             device = input_ids.device
             if getattr(self, "_use_fa2", False):
                 if attention_mask is not None and attention_mask.dim() == 4:
                     logger.warning("4D attention mask ignored under FA2; using full attention")
                     attention_mask = None
             else:
                 if attention_mask is not None and attention_mask.dim() == 4:
                     pass
                 else:
                     dtype = next(self.transformer.parameters()).dtype
                     mask_4d = torch.zeros(B, 1, L, L, dtype=dtype, device=device)
                     if attention_mask is not None and attention_mask.dim() == 2:
                         pad_mask = (1.0 - attention_mask.float()).unsqueeze(1).unsqueeze(2)
                         mask_4d = mask_4d + pad_mask * torch.finfo(dtype).min
                     attention_mask = mask_4d
             outputs = self.transformer(input_ids=input_ids, attention_mask=attention_mask, **kwargs)
             return outputs.logits
     ```

   - **`scripts/pretrain.py`** — Added `--attn_implementation` CLI flag
     ```python
     attn_implementation: str = field(
         default="eager",
         metadata={"help": "Attention backend: eager | sdpa | flash_attention_2."},
     )
     ```
     And in main():
     ```python
     model_config = ModelConfig(
         model_name_or_path=script_args.model_name_or_path,
         init_from_pretrained=True,
         dtype=script_args.dtype,
         attn_implementation=script_args.attn_implementation,
         use_lora=script_args.use_lora,
         lora_rank=script_args.lora_rank,
     )
     ```

   - **`configs/training/finance-qwen3.yaml`** — Previous training config (read-only)
     - Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B, masked diffusion, linear schedule, block_size=128, batch=2, grad_accum=8, LR=1e-4, cosine, 200 warmup

   - **`docs/training-recipes.md`** — Training hyperparameter reference
     - AR adaptation LR for 600M: 3e-5 (not 1e-4)
     - All papers use AdamW, weight_decay=0.1
     - Warmup 500 steps for adaptation

   - **`docs/research.md`** — MPS-specific research (read-only)
   - **`docs/training-configs-raw.md`** — Raw configs from LLaDA, MDLM, BD3LM, Dream, d1, Fast-dLLM
   - **`docs/financial_datasets.md`** — Dataset options (ashraq/financial-news-articles chosen)
   - **`docs/research-fa2-bidirectional.md`** — FA2 research findings (created by researcher agent)

   - **`src/diffusion_lm/diffusion/masked.py`** — Reviewed, confirmed correct ELBO-weighted loss
   - **`src/diffusion_lm/trainers/base.py`** — Reviewed, DiffusionTrainer with NanLossCallback
   - **`src/diffusion_lm/models/masked_diffusion_lm.py`** — Reviewed, double backbone init issue noted but not fixed
   - **`src/diffusion_lm/data/pretraining.py`** — tokenize_and_group processes articles one-by-one (serial, slow for 306k articles)
   - **`src/diffusion_lm/config/model.py`** — ModelConfig with attn_implementation default "eager"

     - `checkpoints/finance-qwen3-adamw/checkpoint-500/trainer_state.json` — Much higher losses (59→22), batch_size=1

   - **Wrong Docker image tag**: `runpod/pytorch:2.8.0-py3.12-cuda12.8.1-cudnn-devel-ubuntu22.04` doesn't exist. User reported error from RunPod logs. 1→# diffusion-lm
     2→
     3→A training framework for diffusion-based language models (dLLMs) supporting masked diffusion (LLaDA/Mercury style), block diffusion (BD3LM), and continuous embedding diffusion.
     4→
     5→Wraps any HuggingFace causal LM backbone with bidirectional attention and adds a full training and inference pipeline. Supports AR-to-dLLM adaptation, SFT, DPO, GRPO, and generation via First-Hitting Sampler.
     6→
     7→## Features
     8→
     9→- **AR-to-dLLM adaptation** — start from any HF causal LM checkpoint (~500x less compute than training from scratch)
    10→- **Masked diffusion** (LLaDA/Mercury style) with ELBO-weighted cross-entropy loss and antithetic timestep sampling
    11→- **Block diffusion** (BD3LM, ICLR 2025 Oral) — AR over blocks, masked within; enables KV caching and 13% PPL improvement
    12→- **First-Hitting Sampler** — theoretically correct, 20x faster than naive categorical sampling; default for all generation
    13→- **Running Confidence Remasking** — prevents Answer Backslide (9.8% MATH-500 failure rate without it)
    14→- **GRPO alignment** (diffu-GRPO, arXiv:2504.12216) — first working RL pipeline for dLLMs
    15→- **DPO alignment** — ELBO-based log-likelihood estimation with shared antithetic timesteps
    16→- **Explicit 4D attention mask** — compatible with Transformers 5.10+, `torch.compile`-safe
    17→- **HuggingFace Trainer extension** — inherits distributed training (DDP/FSDP/DeepSpeed), checkpointing, W&B logging
    18→
    19→## Validated Models
    20→
    21→The framework has been tested with the following backbones:
    22→
    23→| Model | Params | Notes |
    24→|-------|--------|-------|
    25→| `Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B` | 0.6B | Recommended default. Pre-trained diffusion checkpoint available at `dllm-collection/Qwen3-0.6B-diffusion-mdlm-v0.1` for benchmarking. Requires `transformers>=4.51`. |
    26→| `Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B` | 0.5B | Same family as Dream-v0 and Tiny-A2D (validated diffusion paths). Apache 2.0. |
    27→| `meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B` | 1B | Same family as DiffuLLaMA (ICLR 2025). GQA works correctly with 4D mask. Requires HF token. |
    28→| `gpt2` | 124M | Fastest for smoke tests. Pre-validated via DiffuGPT (ICLR 2025). |
    29→
    30→**Avoid** Mistral and Phi-3-mini — both use sliding window attention that operates below the 4D mask injection level.
    31→
    32→## Installation
    33→
    34→```bash
    35→git clone https://github.com/sjonas50/diffusion.git
    36→cd diffusion
    37→uv sync
    38→```
    39→
    40→Requires Python 3.11+. For Flash Attention 2 (recommended for GPU training):
    41→
    42→```bash
    43→pip install flash-attn --no-build-isolation
    44→```
    45→
    46→## Quickstart
    47→
    48→### 1. Pretrain / AR-to-dLLM adaptation
    49→
    50→Fine-tune Qwen3-0.6B on financial news with the masked diffusion objective:
    51→
    52→```bash
    53→uv run python scripts/pretrain.py \
    54→  --model_name_or_path Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B \
    55→  --process_type masked \
    56→  --schedule_type linear \
    57→  --max_steps 100000 \
    58→  --per_device_train_batch_size 4 \
    59→  --gradient_accumulation_steps 8 \
    60→  --learning_rate 3e-4 \
    61→  --output_dir ./checkpoints/pretrain
    62→```
    63→
    64→### 2. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
    65→
    66→```bash
    67→uv run python scripts/sft.py \
    68→  --model_path ./checkpoints/pretrain \
    69→  --dataset_name your_dataset \
    70→  --max_steps 5000 \
    71→  --output_dir ./checkpoints/sft
    72→```
    73→
    74→### 3. GRPO Alignment (recommended for reasoning)
    75→
    76→```bash
    77→uv run python scripts/grpo.py \
    78→  --model_path ./checkpoints/sft \
    79→  --max_steps 500 \
    80→  --group_size 8 \
    81→  --output_dir ./checkpoints/grpo
    82→```
    83→
    84→### 4. Generate
    85→
    86→```bash
    87→uv run python scripts/generate.py \
    88→  --model_path ./checkpoints/grpo \
    89→  --prompt "The Federal Reserve announced" \
    90→  --sampler first_hitting \
    91→  --num_steps 64 \
    92→  --max_new_tokens 128
    93→```
    94→
    95→Available samplers: `first_hitting` (default), `block`, `continuous`, `cached`.
    96→
    97→### 5. Evaluate
    98→
    99→```bash
   100→uv run python scripts/evaluate.py \
   101→  --model_path ./checkpoints/grpo \
   102→  --benchmarks perplexity \
   103→  --dataset wikitext \
   104→  --dataset_config wikitext-2-raw-v1 \
   105→  --split test
   106→```
   107→
   108→## Finance Domain Example
   109→
   110→The framework has been tested end-to-end on `ashraq/financial-news-articles` (306k Reuters/Bloomberg articles) with Qwen3-0.6B:
   111→
   112→```python
   113→from datasets import load_dataset
   114→from transformers import AutoTokenizer
   115→from diffusion_lm.data.pretraining import tokenize_and_group
   116→
   117→tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B")
   118→raw_ds = load_dataset("ashraq/financial-news-articles", split="train")
   119→chunks = list(tokenize_and_group(raw_ds, tokenizer, block_size=512, text_column="text"))
   120→```
   121→
   122→Other recommended finance datasets:
   123→- `PleIAs/SEC` — 373k SEC 10-K annual reports, CC0 license (use streaming for full dataset)
   124→- `sujet-ai/Sujet-Finance-Instruct-177k` — 177k QA pairs for SFT, Apache 2.0
   125→
   126→## Testing
   127→
   128→```bash
   129→# Fast unit tests (54 tests, ~4s)
   130→uv run pytest tests/ -m "not slow"
   131→
   132→# Integration tests against real model weights (downloads ~1-2GB per model)
   133→uv run pytest tests/test_model_compat.py -v -m slow
   134→```
   135→
   136→The integration tests verify bidirectionality, forward pass sanity, generation, and mask token handling for each supported model.
   137→
   138→## Docker
   139→
   140→```bash
   141→# Build
   142→docker build -t diffusion-lm .
   143→
   144→# Train with GPU
   145→docker compose run train
   146→
   147→# Generate
   148→docker compose run generate
   149→```
   150→
   151→## Project Structure
   152→
   153→```
   154→src/diffusion_lm/
   155→├── config/          # ModelConfig, DiffusionConfig, TrainingArguments, GenerationConfig
   156→├── schedules/       # Linear, cosine, log-linear noise schedules
   157→├── diffusion/       # MaskedDiffusionProcess, BlockDiffusionProcess, ContinuousDiffusionProcess
   158→├── models/          # BidirectionalTransformer backbone, MaskedDiffusionLM, ContinuousDiffusionLM
   159→├── data/            # Collators (RandomTruncate, SFT, DPO), PretrainingDataset, SFTDataset
   160→├── trainers/        # DiffusionTrainer, SFTTrainer, DiffusionDPOTrainer, DiffusionGRPOTrainer
   161→├── samplers/        # FirstHittingSampler, BlockSampler, ContinuousSampler, CachedSampler
   162→└── evaluation/      # ELBOPerplexity, DiffusionLMEvalAdapter
   163→scripts/             # pretrain.py, sft.py, dpo.py, grpo.py, generate.py, evaluate.py
   164→configs/models/      # qwen3-0-6b.yaml, qwen2-5-0-5b.yaml, llama-3-2-1b.yaml, small.yaml, ...
   165→tests/               # 54 unit tests + 12 slow integration tests across all supported models
   166→```
   167→
   168→## Key Design Decisions
   169→
   170→**Explicit 4D attention mask** — rather than monkey-patching `_update_causal_mask` (removed in Transformers 5.10), we inject an all-zeros float mask of shape `(B, 1, L, L)` directly in `forward()`. This is `torch.compile`-safe and works across all HF model architectures.
   171→
   172→**Antithetic timestep sampling** — draw one `u ~ U[0,1)`, set `t_i = (u + i/B) mod 1`. Reduces gradient variance ~B-fold at zero cost.
   173→
   174→**ELBO-weighted loss** — divide CE by `p_mask(t)` at each position. Mandatory for correct ELBO estimation; omitting it biases training.
   175→
   176→**First-Hitting Sampler as default** — standard categorical sampling exploits a mathematical inaccuracy that inflates benchmarks. The First-Hitting Sampler is theoretically correct and ~20x faster.
   177→
   178→**Tied weight handling** — GPT-2, Qwen, and LLaMA all share `embed_tokens.weight` with `lm_head.weight`. `DiffusionTrainer._save()` temporarily breaks this tie before writing safetensors checkpoints and restores it immediately after.
   179→
   180→## References
   181→
   182→- [LLaDA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.09992) — Large Language Diffusion with mAsking
   183→- [Mercury 2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.17298) — Inception Labs, 1000+ tok/s on H100
   184→- [BD3LM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.09573) — Block Diffusion, ICLR 2025 Oral
   185→- [diffu-GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.12216) — First RL pipeline for dLLMs
   186→- [First-Hitting Sampler](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.02908) — Correct and fast sampling
   187→- [MDLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07524) — Masked Diffusion Language Models (NeurIPS 2024)
   188→- [DiffuLLaMA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.17891) — LLaMA-family AR-to-dLLM adaptation (ICLR 2025)
   189→

1→# Diffusion LLM Training Framework
     2→
     3→A training framework for diffusion-based language models (dLLMs) supporting masked diffusion
     4→(LLaDA/Mercury style), block diffusion (BD3LM), and continuous embedding diffusion. Wraps any
     5→HuggingFace causal LM backbone with bidirectional attention via explicit 4D mask injection, then
     6→adds a full training and inference pipeline. Supports AR-to-dLLM adaptation (recommended init
     7→path: ~500x less compute than from scratch), SFT, DPO, GRPO, and generation via First-Hitting
     8→Sampler (20x faster, theoretically correct).
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Commands
    13→
    14→### Build / Lint
    15→```bash
    16→uv run ruff check . --fix && uv run ruff format .
    17→```
    18→
    19→### Test
    20→```bash
    21→uv run pytest tests/ -v
    22→```
    23→
    24→### Test (fast, no slow integration tests)
    25→```bash
    26→uv run pytest tests/ -v -m "not slow"
    27→```
    28→
    29→### Lint only (no fix)
    30→```bash
    31→uv run ruff check .
    32→```
    33→
    34→### Pre-commit (all checks)
    35→```bash
    36→uv run pre-commit run --all-files
    37→```
    38→
    39→### Smoke test (pretrain, AR-to-dLLM adaptation from GPT-2)
    40→```bash
    41→uv run python scripts/pretrain.py \
    42→  --model_name_or_path gpt2 \
    43→  --init_from_pretrained true \
    44→  --process_type masked \
    45→  --schedule_type linear \
    46→  --max_steps 100 \
    47→  --per_device_train_batch_size 4 \
    48→  --output_dir ./checkpoints/smoke-test
    49→```
    50→
    51→### Generate (First-Hitting Sampler, default)
    52→```bash
    53→uv run python scripts/generate.py \
    54→  --model_path ./checkpoints/smoke-test \
    55→  --prompt "The meaning of life is" \
    56→  --sampler first_hitting \
    57→  --num_steps 64 \
    58→  --max_new_tokens 128
    59→```
    60→
    61→### GRPO alignment (preferred over DPO for reasoning)
    62→```bash
    63→uv run python scripts/grpo.py \
    64→  --model_path ./checkpoints/sft \
    65→  --reward_model_path ./checkpoints/reward \
    66→  --max_steps 500
    67→```
    68→
    69→---
    70→
    71→## Current Build Phase
    72→
    73→**Phase 0: Project Scaffold** — not started
    74→
    75→Track progress in `docs/build-plan.md`. Do not advance to the next phase until the gate test for
    76→the current phase passes.
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## Stack
    81→
    82→| Tool | Version | Purpose |
    83→|------|---------|---------|
    84→| Python | 3.11+ | Required for modern type hint syntax (`X \| Y`, `Self`) |
    85→| uv | latest | Package management, virtual environments |
    86→| PyTorch | >=2.2 | Tensor ops, autograd, distributed |
    87→| HuggingFace Transformers | >=4.40,<5.10 | Backbone models, Trainer, tokenizers — pin upper bound |
    88→| HuggingFace datasets | >=2.18 | Streaming dataset loading |
    89→| flash-attn | >=2.5 | FA2 kernel — `is_causal=False` passed explicitly |
    90→| accelerate | >=0.28 | FSDP/DeepSpeed integration |
    91→| peft | >=0.10 | LoRA adapters |
    92→| lm-evaluation-harness | >=0.4 | Benchmarks |
    93→| wandb | >=0.16 | Experiment tracking |
    94→| loguru | >=0.7 | Structured logging |
    95→| ruff | >=0.4 | Linting and formatting |
    96→| pytest | >=8.0 | Testing |
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→## Key Architectural Decisions — Do Not Revisit
   101→
   102→These decisions are final. Do not second-guess them when implementing.
   103→
   104→1. **Use HF Trainer, not a custom training loop.** `DiffusionTrainer` extends `HFTrainer` and
   105→   overrides only `compute_loss()`. All gradient accumulation, mixed precision, distributed
   106→   training, checkpointing, and logging are inherited.
   107→
   108→2. **Bidirectional attention via explicit 4D mask injection — NOT monkey-patch.**
   109→   `_update_causal_mask` is deprecated and removed in Transformers v5.10. The new
   110→   `masking_utils.create_causal_mask` path breaks `torch.compile(fullgraph=True)` (issue #42950).
   111→   The Gemma3 bidirectional mask bug (issue #39389) proves silent causal fallback is a real failure
   112→   mode — no crash, just wrong attention and degraded quality.
   113→   **Correct approach:** `BidirectionalTransformer.forward()` builds an explicit all-zeros 4D float
   114→   mask `(B, 1, L, L)` and passes it directly. Bypasses all HF internals. FA2: `is_causal=False`
   115→   passed explicitly. Do NOT use `AutoModelForMaskedLM` — smaller ecosystem.
   116→
   117→3. **AR-to-dLLM adaptation as the default init path.** `ModelConfig.init_from_pretrained=True`
   118→   by default. Start from a pretrained AR checkpoint, fine-tune with masked diffusion objective
   119→   on ~1B tokens. ~500x less compute than training from scratch. Validated by Dream 7B (Aug 2025)
   120→   and Fast-dLLM v2 (Oct 2025).
   121→
   122→4. **First-Hitting Sampler as the default sampler (not confidence-based categorical).**
   123→   Standard categorical sampling exploits a mathematical inaccuracy (arXiv:2409.02908) that
   124→   inflates benchmarks. First-Hitting Sampler is theoretically correct, 20x faster, drop-in
   125→   replacement. Assigns each masked token an independent first-hitting time from the score
   126→   function, reveals in order. Default: `GenerationConfig.sampler = "first_hitting"`.
   127→
   128→5. **No timestep input to the model (masked diffusion).** RADD paper proves this is theoretically
   129→   unnecessary. Omitting it means any HF CausalLM can be used as-is. `ContinuousDiffusionLM` is
   130→   the sole exception.
   131→
   132→6. **ELBO-weighted loss is mandatory.** Dividing CE loss by `p_mask(t)` is required for correct
   133→   training — not optional. Without it the model is undertrained at low-noise timesteps.
   134→
   135→7. **Antithetic timestep sampling for every batch.** `t_i = (u + i/B) mod 1`. For DPO/GRPO, use
   136→   the SAME timestep draw for both policy and reference model.
   137→
   138→8. **GRPO preferred over DPO for reasoning tasks.** diffu-GRPO (arXiv:2504.12216) is the first
   139→   working RL pipeline for dLLMs. LLaDA-8B + diffu-GRPO matches AR reasoning models on
   140→   GSM8K/MATH500. Use `DiffusionGRPOTrainer` for reasoning alignment.
   141→
   142→9. **src layout.** All importable code under `src/diffusion_lm/`. Scripts in `scripts/` are entry
   143→   points only. Tests in `tests/` import from `diffusion_lm` (installed via `uv pip install -e .`).
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## Critical Implementation Gotchas
   148→
   149→### Causal mask — use explicit 4D mask, NOT monkey-patch
   150→```python
   151→# WRONG — deprecated, removed in v5.10, breaks torch.compile:
   152→self.transformer.model._update_causal_mask = lambda *a, **kw: None
   153→
   154→# CORRECT — explicit 4D zeros mask bypasses all HF mask generation:
   155→def forward(self, input_ids, attention_mask=None, **kwargs):
   156→    B, L = input_ids.shape
   157→    if attention_mask is None or attention_mask.dim() != 4:
   158→        # All-zeros float mask = no masking = full bidirectional attention
   159→        attention_mask = torch.zeros(
   160→            B, 1, L, L, dtype=self.transformer.dtype, device=input_ids.device
   161→        )
   162→    return self.transformer(input_ids=input_ids, attention_mask=attention_mask, **kwargs).logits
   163→```
   164→Verify bidirectionality: changing token at position 5 must change logits at position 3.
   165→
   166→### Flash Attention 2 — pass is_causal explicitly
   167→```python
   168→# FA2 requires explicit is_causal=False — do NOT rely on config propagation:
   169→flash_attn_func(q, k, v, causal=False)
   170→```
   171→
   172→### Loss normalization
   173→```python
   174→# Correct:
   175→ce = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, V), x0.view(-1), reduction='none').view(x0.shape)
   176→weighted = ce / p_mask(t).unsqueeze(-1).clamp(min=1e-5)
   177→loss = (weighted * masked_positions).sum() / masked_positions.sum().clamp(min=1)
   178→
   179→# Wrong (undertrains near-clean steps):
   180→loss = ce[masked_positions].mean()  # no ELBO weighting
   181→```
   182→
   183→### SFT prompt protection — apply BEFORE backbone, AFTER forward_process
   184→```python
   185→corrupted, mask = diffusion.forward_process(input_ids, t)
   186→corrupted = torch.where(prompt_mask, input_ids, corrupted)  # restore prompt tokens
   187→logits = backbone(corrupted, attention_mask)
   188→loss = diffusion.compute_loss(logits, input_ids, corrupted, t, loss_mask=~prompt_mask)
   189→```
   190→
   191→### Cross-sample attention leakage in packed sequences
   192→When packing multiple sequences into one batch row, the collator MUST emit a per-sample
   193→block-diagonal attention mask. Without it, tokens from different samples attend to each other —
   194→silent quality degradation with no crash signal.
   195→
   196→### "Answer Backslide" prevention
   197→9.8% of MATH-500 failures have correct intermediate tokens overwritten in later steps.
   198→Enable `running_confidence_remasking=True` in `GenerationConfig` (default). Free, no retraining.
   199→
   200→### DPO/GRPO memory requirements
   201→```python
   202→# DPO: ~32 forward passes per step (4 quantities × 8 MC samples)
   203→# GRPO: K=8 rollouts + n_mc=8 per rollout = 64+ forward passes
   204→# Defaults for large models:
   205→training_args = DiffusionTrainingArguments(
   206→    gradient_checkpointing=True,
   207→    bf16=True,
   208→    per_device_train_batch_size=1,
   209→    gradient_accumulation_steps=16,
   210→)
   211→# DPO: cpu_offload_ref=True for reference model
   212→# GRPO: process rollouts sequentially if OOM
   213→```
   214→
   215→### DPO/GRPO antithetic sampling for variance reduction
   216→Use the SAME antithetic timestep `u` for both policy model and reference model when estimating
   217→a single pair. Their log-prob difference has much lower variance with shared timesteps.
   218→
   219→### EOS suppression during generation
   220→```python
   221→# At non-final denoising positions:
   222→logits[:, :, eos_token_id] = float('-inf')  # before argmax
   223→```
   224→
   225→### Continuous diffusion rounding
   226→- **Training:** straight-through estimator — `x_rounded = x + (x_rounded - x).detach()`
   227→- **Inference:** exact nearest-neighbor — `torch.cdist(predicted_emb, embedding_table).argmin(-1)`
   228→
   229→### mask_token_id must be set before training
   230→`DiffusionConfig.mask_token_id` defaults to `-1` (invalid). `DiffusionTrainer.__init__` must
   231→raise `ValueError` if still `-1`. Always call `add_mask_token(model, tokenizer)` first.
   232→
   233→### LogLinear schedule numerical stability
   234→```python
   235→alpha = torch.exp(-(-torch.log1p(-(1 - eps) * t))).clamp(0.0, 1.0)
   236→```
   237→
   238→### NaN crashes at scale
   239→LLaDA crashed at 1.2T/2.3T tokens without mitigation. Defaults:
   240→- `max_grad_norm=1.0` (clip gradient norm)
   241→- Checkpoint every 1000 steps
   242→- LR reduction at first NaN (handled by `NanLossCallback`)
   243→
   244→### Transformers version pinning
   245→Pin `transformers<5.10` in `pyproject.toml`. The removal of `_update_causal_mask` in v5.10
   246→does not affect our explicit 4D mask approach, but other internal changes may. Bump only
   247→after explicit compatibility testing.
   248→
   249→---
   250→
   251→## File Structure
   252→
   253→```
   254→diffusion/
   255→├── CLAUDE.md                          # This file
   256→├── pyproject.toml                     # Single config: deps, ruff, pytest
   257→├── .env.example                       # Template for secrets
   258→├── .gitignore
   259→├── .pre-commit-config.yaml
   260→├── Dockerfile
   261→├── docker-compose.yml
   262→├── README.md
   263→│
   264→├── src/
   265→│   └── diffusion_lm/
   266→│       ├── __init__.py
   267→│       ├── config/
   268→│       │   ├── model.py               # ModelConfig (init_from_pretrained=True default)
   269→│       │   ├── diffusion.py           # DiffusionConfig (process_type, block_size)
   270→│       │   ├── training.py            # DiffusionTrainingArguments (DPO + GRPO params)
   271→│       │   └── generation.py         # GenerationConfig (sampler="first_hitting" default)
   272→│       ├── schedules/
   273→│       │   ├── base.py               # NoiseSchedule ABC
   274→│       │   ├── linear.py             # alpha(t) = 1 - t
   275→│       │   ├── cosine.py             # alpha(t) = cos(pi/2 * t)
   276→│       │   └── loglinear.py          # alpha(t) = exp(-sigma(t))
   277→│       ├── diffusion/
   278→│       │   ├── base.py               # DiffusionProcess ABC

   286→│       ├── data/

   340→
   341→---
   342→
   343→## Reference Implementations
   344→
   345→When in doubt, consult these before inventing solutions:
   346→
   347→- LLaDA (masked diffusion, 8B, MIT): https://github.com/ML-GSAI/LLaDA
   348→- dLLM unified library (Feb 2026): https://github.com/ZHZisZZ/dllm
   349→- BD3LM block diffusion (ICLR 2025 Oral): https://github.com/kuleshov-group/bd3lms
   350→- d1 diffu-GRPO (RL for dLLMs): https://github.com/dllm-reasoning/d1
   351→- MDLM (NeurIPS 2024, log-linear schedule): https://github.com/kuleshov-group/mdlm
   352→- Mercury 1 paper (generation speed): https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.17298
   353→- First-Hitting Sampler paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.02908

   355→

1→# Diffusion LLM Project Memory
     2→
     3→## Project Goal
     4→Build a training framework for diffusion-based language models (dLLMs), supporting both masked diffusion (LLaDA/Mercury style) and continuous embedding diffusion.
     5→
     6→## Key Technical Facts
     7→- Diffusion LLMs use the same Transformer backbone as autoregressive LLMs, just with bidirectional attention (no causal mask)
     8→- Masked diffusion: variable-rate token masking, CE loss divided by p_mask(t) = unbiased ELBO
     9→- Timestep t does NOT need to be input to the model (proven by RADD + arXiv:2409.02908)
    10→- **DO NOT use `_update_causal_mask` monkey-patch** — deprecated, removed in Transformers v5.10, breaks torch.compile. Use explicit 4D float zeros mask `(batch, 1, seq_len, seq_len)` injected in forward() instead.
    11→- For Flash Attention 2: pass `is_causal=False` explicitly to `flash_attn_func`
    12→- LLaDA trained 8B params on 2.3T tokens, competitive with LLaMA-3 8B
    13→- Mercury 2 (Feb 24, 2026): 727–1196 tok/s (Blackwell), TTFT 3.48s, AIME 91.1, $0.25/$0.75 per M tokens. Closed/proprietary.
    14→- **AR-to-dLLM adaptation is now dominant** — fine-tune pretrained AR checkpoint with ~1B diffusion tokens (~500x less compute than from scratch)
    15→- First-Hitting Sampler (arXiv:2409.02908): 20x faster inference, theoretically correct. Replaces categorical sampling.
    16→- Running Confidence Remasking: free inference-time fix for "Answer Backslide" (9.8% MATH-500 failure rate)
    17→- Block Diffusion / BD3LM (arXiv:2503.09573, ICLR 2025 Oral): AR over blocks + masked diffusion within blocks. Unlocks KV caching, 13% perplexity gain.
    18→- diffu-GRPO (arXiv:2504.12216): First working RL pipeline for dLLMs. LLaDA-8B matches AR reasoning models on GSM8K/MATH500.
    19→
    20→## Framework Stack
    21→- PyTorch + HuggingFace Transformers/Trainer (pin version — breaking changes in v5.10)
    22→- Extends HF Trainer for automatic distributed training support
    23→- Config via HF-style dataclasses + HfArgumentParser
    24→
    25→## Key References
    26→- LLaDA: github.com/ML-GSAI/LLaDA (MIT license)
    27→- MDLM: github.com/kuleshov-group/mdlm (NeurIPS 2024)
    28→- dLLM unified library: github.com/ZHZisZZ/dllm (arXiv:2602.22661)
    29→- BD3LM: github.com/kuleshov-group/bd3lms (arXiv:2503.09573)
    30→- Fast-dLLM: github.com/NVlabs/Fast-dLLM (NVIDIA Research license — verify before use)
    31→- d1 diffu-GRPO: github.com/dllm-reasoning/d1 (arXiv:2504.12216)
    32→- Mercury 1 paper: arxiv.org/abs/2506.17298
    33→- First-Hitting Sampler: arxiv.org/abs/2409.02908
    34→
    35→## Project Files
    36→- plan.md — detailed implementation plan (updated March 2026)
    37→- docs/research.md — full research synthesis with sources
    38→- docs/architecture.md — component diagram and data flows
    39→- docs/build-plan.md — phased build plan with test gates
    40→- CLAUDE.md — project-specific instructions and gotchas
    41→

1→# Research: Training Recipes and Hyperparameters for Diffusion LLMs
     2→
     3→## Executive Summary
     4→
     5→Across all major dLLM papers (LLaDA, MDLM, Dream, Fast-dLLM v2, d1/diffu-GRPO), the optimizer is universally AdamW with standard betas. Peak learning rates range from 3e-4 (small models, from scratch) down to 1e-5 (7B+ adaptation). The dominant training paradigm has shifted from from-scratch pretraining to AR-to-dLLM adaptation, requiring only ~1-3B tokens at reduced LR (1-2e-5). Training stability is the primary unsolved risk: LLaDA crashed at 1.2T/2.3T tokens, requiring LR reduction from 4e-4 to 1e-4. MPS/Apple Silicon is viable for small-scale development but has hard limitations on Flash Attention and distributed training.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Problem Statement
    10→
    11→Building a dLLM training framework requires concrete hyperparameter choices across pretraining, adaptation, SFT, and RL (GRPO/DPO). Vague guidance like "use AdamW" is insufficient. This document extracts exact numbers from papers, codebases, and configs to provide copy-paste-ready training recipes for model sizes from 125M to 8B.
    12→
    13→---
    14→
    15→## Optimizer Choice
    16→
    17→### Consensus: AdamW everywhere
    18→
    19→Every major dLLM paper uses AdamW. No paper uses Adafactor, Lion, SOAP, or other alternatives.
    20→
    21→| Paper | Optimizer | beta1 | beta2 | eps | Weight Decay |
    22→|-------|-----------|-------|-------|-----|-------------|
    23→| LLaDA 8B | AdamW | 0.9 (implied) | 0.999 (implied) | not stated | 0.1 |
    24→| MDLM 110M | AdamW | 0.9 | 0.999 | default | 0.0 |
    25→| Dream 7B | AdamW (implied) | not disclosed | not disclosed | not disclosed | not disclosed |
    26→| Fast-dLLM v2 | AdamW | not stated | not stated | not stated | not stated |
    27→| d1/diffu-GRPO | AdamW | 0.9 | 0.99 | default | 0.1 |
    28→
    29→**Key observation:** MDLM uses zero weight decay — unusual but consistent across their experiments. LLaDA and d1 use 0.1 weight decay, which is standard for large Transformer training.
    30→
    31→### Memory-efficient optimizers
    32→
    33→No dLLM paper uses 8-bit Adam or paged optimizers. However, for single-GPU or memory-constrained setups, `bitsandbytes` 8-bit AdamW is a drop-in replacement that cuts optimizer state memory by 75% with <2% accuracy loss (validated on LLM training at scale via MLPerf 2025). The d1/diffu-GRPO codebase uses 4-bit QLoRA quantization for the base model, which achieves similar memory savings through a different mechanism.
    34→
    35→**Recommendation for this framework:**
    36→- Default: `torch.optim.AdamW` with beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999, weight_decay=0.1
    37→- Memory-constrained: `bitsandbytes.optim.AdamW8bit` as opt-in flag
    38→- For GRPO: beta2=0.99 (following d1), which provides slightly faster adaptation to reward signal changes
    39→
    40→---
    41→
    42→## Learning Rate Schedules
    43→
    44→### From-scratch pretraining
    45→
    46→| Paper | Model Size | Peak LR | Schedule | Warmup |
    47→|-------|-----------|---------|----------|--------|
    48→| LLaDA | 8B | 4e-4 | Warmup-Stable-Decay (WSD) | 2000 steps linear |
    49→| MDLM | 110M | 3e-4 | Constant with warmup | not specified |
    50→| BD3LM | 110M | 3e-4 (same codebase as MDLM) | Constant with warmup | not specified |
    51→
    52→**LLaDA's WSD schedule in detail:**
    53→1. Linear warmup: 0 -> 4e-4 over 2000 iterations
    54→2. Stable phase: 4e-4 held for 1.2T tokens
    55→3. Decay phase 1: Reduced to 1e-4 for 0.8T tokens (crash recovery)
    56→4. Decay phase 2: Linear decay 1e-4 -> 1e-5 over final 0.3T tokens
    57→
    58→The WSD schedule is notable because the "decay phase 1" was an emergency intervention after a NaN crash, not a planned schedule change. The intended schedule was likely WSD with a single decay phase.
    59→
    60→### AR-to-dLLM adaptation
    61→
    62→| Paper | Base Model | Peak LR | Tokens | Schedule |
    63→|-------|-----------|---------|--------|----------|
    64→| Fast-dLLM v2 | Qwen2.5-1.5B | 2e-5 | ~3.15B (6000 steps) | Linear warmup 500 steps |
    65→| Fast-dLLM v2 | Qwen2.5-7B | 1e-5 | ~1.31B (2500 steps) | Linear warmup 500 steps |
    66→| Dream 7B | Qwen2.5-7B | not disclosed | 580B | not disclosed |
    67→
    68→**Critical insight:** Adaptation LR is 20-40x lower than from-scratch LR. Fast-dLLM v2 uses 1-2e-5 for adaptation vs 3-4e-4 for from-scratch. Dream's paper states "learning rate plays a critical role in preserving the beneficial properties inherited from AR initialization" but withholds the actual value.
    69→
    70→### SFT
    71→
    72→| Paper | Base | Peak LR | Schedule | Warmup | Epochs |
    73→|-------|------|---------|----------|--------|--------|
    74→| LLaDA SFT | LLaDA-8B-Base | 2.5e-5 | WSD | 50 steps linear | 3 epochs on 4.5M pairs |
    75→| Dream SFT | Dream-7B-Base | 2e-6 | not specified | not specified | 3 epochs on 1.8M pairs |
    76→
    77→**Dream SFT is 10x lower LR than LLaDA SFT.** This likely reflects Dream's use of CART (Context-Adaptive Reweighted Timestep) loss weighting, which changes the effective gradient magnitude.
    78→
    79→### GRPO/RL
    80→
    81→| Paper | Base | Peak LR | Schedule |
    82→|-------|------|---------|----------|
    83→| d1/diffu-GRPO | LLaDA-8B-Instruct | 3e-6 | Constant with warmup |
    84→
    85→**Recommendation for this framework:**
    86→
    87→| Stage | 125M | 600M | 1.3B | 8B |
    88→|-------|------|------|------|-----|
    89→| From-scratch pretrain | 3e-4 | 2e-4 | 1.5e-4 | 4e-4 (LLaDA) |
    90→| AR adaptation | 5e-5 | 3e-5 | 2e-5 | 1e-5 |
    91→| SFT | 5e-5 | 1e-5 | 5e-6 | 2e-6 |
    92→| GRPO | 1e-5 | 5e-6 | 3e-6 | 3e-6 |
    93→
    94→Schedule: WSD (warmup-stable-decay) for pretraining, cosine decay for SFT/GRPO. Warmup: 2000 steps for pretraining, 500 steps for adaptation, 50-100 steps for SFT/GRPO.
    95→
    96→---
    97→
    98→## Batch Size and Gradient Accumulation
    99→
   100→| Paper | Model Size | Global Batch Size | Per-GPU Batch | Seq Length | Tokens/Batch |
   101→|-------|-----------|-------------------|---------------|------------|-------------|
   102→| LLaDA | 8B | 1280 | 4 | 4096 | 5.24M |
   103→| MDLM | 110M | 512 | varies | 1024 | 524K |
   104→| Fast-dLLM v2 | 1.5B/7B | 256 | varies | 2048 | 524K |
   105→| Dream SFT | 7B | 8/GPU * 8 GPUs = 64 | 8 | 2048 | 131K |
   106→| d1/diffu-GRPO | 8B | 12 (6 * 2 grad accum) | 6 | varies | varies |
   107→
   108→**Scaling pattern:** ~500K tokens per batch is the sweet spot for models up to 1.5B. LLaDA scales to 5.24M tokens/batch at 8B, which is aggressive but consistent with AR LLM pretraining norms. GRPO uses tiny batch sizes (12) because each "sample" requires K=6 rollouts with 128 diffusion steps each.
   109→
   110→**Recommendation:** Start with global_batch_size=512 for 125M, scale to 1024-2048 for 1.3B+. For GRPO, batch size is constrained by rollout memory — use gradient_accumulation_steps to compensate.
   111→
   112→---
   113→
   114→## AR-to-dLLM Adaptation Details
   115→
   116→This is the most important section for practical training. From-scratch pretraining is largely obsolete for this framework.
   117→
   118→### Fast-dLLM v2 recipe (most detailed, recommended starting point)
   119→
   120→- **Base models:** Qwen2.5-1.5B, Qwen2.5-7B
   121→- **Mechanism:** Block diffusion with complementary attention mask
   122→- **Block size:** 32 (fixed), sub-block size 8
   123→- **Optimizer:** AdamW
   124→- **LR:** 2e-5 (1.5B), 1e-5 (7B)
   125→- **Warmup:** 500 steps linear
   126→- **Batch size:** 256 sequences * 2048 tokens = 524K tokens/step
   127→- **Total tokens:** ~3.15B (1.5B model), ~1.31B (7B model)
   128→- **Total steps:** 6000 (1.5B), 2500 (7B)
   129→- **Hardware:** 64x A100, DeepSpeed Zero-3
   130→- **Wall time:** ~8h (1.5B), ~12h (7B)
   131→
   132→### Dream recipe (less detailed)
   133→
   134→- **Base model:** Qwen2.5-7B (as Dream-v0-Base-7B)
   135→- **Training corpus:** 580B tokens (NOT adaptation — full pretraining with AR init)
   136→- **SFT data:** 1.8M instruction pairs from Tulu 3 + SmolLM 2, 3 epochs
   137→- **SFT LR:** 2e-6
   138→- **SFT per-GPU batch:** 8, micro-batch with gradient checkpointing
   139→- **Loss weighting:** CART (context-adaptive reweighted timestep)
   140→
   141→**Key distinction:** Dream trained on 580B tokens, which is NOT the ~1B token adaptation recipe. Dream uses AR initialization but then does extended pretraining. Fast-dLLM v2 is the true "adaptation in 1B tokens" approach.
   142→
   143→### Recommended adaptation recipe for this framework
   144→
   145→```yaml
   146→# configs/training/adapt.yaml
   147→optimizer: adamw
   148→lr: 2e-5           # for 1.5B; use 1e-5 for 7B+
   149→lr_schedule: linear_warmup_cosine_decay
   150→warmup_steps: 500
   151→weight_decay: 0.1
   152→beta1: 0.9
   153→beta2: 0.999
   154→max_grad_norm: 1.0
   155→batch_size: 256     # global, adjust per GPU count
   156→seq_length: 2048
   157→total_steps: 6000   # ~3B tokens for 1.5B model
   158→bf16: true
   159→gradient_checkpointing: true
   160→```
   161→
   162→---
   163→
   164→## Training Stability
   165→
   166→### LLaDA crash analysis
   167→
   168→LLaDA crashed at 1.2T out of 2.3T tokens. The mitigation was:
   169→1. Resume from checkpoint
   170→2. Reduce LR from 4e-4 to 1e-4 (4x reduction)
   171→3. Continue training at reduced LR for 0.8T tokens
   172→4. Linear decay to 1e-5 over final 0.3T tokens
   173→
   174→### ELBO-weighted loss stability
   175→
   176→The ELBO weighting `ce / p_mask(t)` creates numerical instability near t=0 (where p_mask approaches 0). Mitigations:
   177→- **Clamp p_mask:** `p_mask(t).clamp(min=1e-5)` — mandatory
   178→- **Gradient clipping:** max_grad_norm=1.0 (LLaDA default, d1 uses 0.2 for GRPO)
   179→- **NaN detection callback:** Reduce LR by 2x on first NaN, skip batch on subsequent NaN within window
   180→- **Checkpoint frequency:** Every 1000 steps minimum
   181→
   182→### d1/diffu-GRPO stability
   183→
   184→d1 uses aggressive gradient clipping (max_grad_norm=0.2 vs 1.0 for pretraining). This is necessary because GRPO loss has high variance from MC sampling of the diffusion log-probabilities. Additional settings from d1:
   185→- LoRA rank: 128, alpha: 64 (high rank for reasoning tasks)
   186→- LoRA dropout: 0.05
   187→- 4-bit base model quantization (QLoRA)
   188→- K=6 rollouts per prompt
   189→- 12 policy gradient inner update iterations per batch
   190→- Remasking strategy: `low_confidence`
   191→
   192→### Antithetic timestep sampling
   193→
   194→Used by all papers for variance reduction. Implementation:
   195→```python
   196→u = torch.rand(1)
   197→t = (u + torch.arange(B) / B) % 1.0  # antithetic samples
   198→```
   199→For DPO/GRPO: use SAME `u` for policy and reference model.
   200→
   201→---
   202→
   203→## MPS/Apple Silicon Considerations
   204→
   205→### What works
   206→- PyTorch MPS backend supports basic Transformer training
   207→- `torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention` works on MPS with some caveats
   208→- Training small models (125M-600M) is viable for development/debugging
   209→- bitsandbytes has experimental MPS support as of late 2025
   210→
   211→### What does NOT work
   212→- **Flash Attention 2:** Not available on MPS. The `flash-attn` package requires CUDA. Use `sdpa` attention implementation instead.
   213→- **Distributed training:** MPS does not support DDP, FSDP, or DeepSpeed
   214→- **Large sequence lengths:** SDPA on MPS has memory allocation failures above ~12K tokens
   215→- **torch.compile:** Limited support on MPS, `fullgraph=True` mode not reliable
   216→- **bf16:** MPS supports float16 but bf16 support is limited/absent on older chips
   217→
   218→### Recommended MPS development setup
   219→```python
   220→# Use SDPA (not Flash Attention) on MPS
   221→model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
   222→    "gpt2",
   223→    attn_implementation="sdpa",  # NOT "flash_attention_2"
   224→    torch_dtype=torch.float16,   # NOT bf16 on MPS
   225→)
   226→# Explicit 4D mask approach works fine on MPS — no FA2 dependency
   227→```
   228→
   229→### MLX alternative
   230→Apple's MLX framework outperforms PyTorch MPS by 2-3x for inference. However, MLX lacks HF Trainer integration, making it unsuitable for a framework built on HF Trainer. Use PyTorch MPS for development, target CUDA for production training.
   231→
   232→---
   233→
   234→## Recommended Default Stack
   235→
   236→```toml
   237→# pyproject.toml training defaults
   238→[tool.diffusion_lm.training_defaults]
   239→optimizer = "adamw"
   240→lr = "3e-4"                    # from-scratch; override for adaptation/SFT
   241→lr_schedule = "warmup_stable_decay"
   242→warmup_steps = 2000
   243→weight_decay = 0.1
   244→beta1 = 0.9
   245→beta2 = 0.999
   246→max_grad_norm = 1.0
   247→global_batch_size = 512
   248→seq_length = 2048
   249→bf16 = true
   250→gradient_checkpointing = true
   251→checkpoint_every_steps = 1000
   252→```
   253→
   254→---
   255→
   256→## Open Questions
   257→
   258→1. **Dream's pretraining LR:** Dream 7B trained on 580B tokens but withholds optimizer hyperparameters. Their SFT LR (2e-6) is 10x lower than LLaDA's (2.5e-5) — is this because of CART loss weighting or a fundamentally different training dynamic?
   259→
   260→2. **Optimal adaptation token count:** Fast-dLLM v2 uses 1-3B tokens, but Dream uses 580B. Is the 500x compute savings real, or does Fast-dLLM v2's block diffusion approach require less adaptation by design?
   261→
   262→3. **8-bit optimizer impact on ELBO loss:** No dLLM paper has tested 8-bit AdamW. The ELBO-weighted loss divides by small p_mask values — quantized optimizer states may interact poorly with these extreme gradient magnitudes. Needs empirical testing.
   263→
   264→4. **MPS bf16 support timeline:** Apple has not committed to bf16 on MPS. This forces float16 on Apple Silicon, which may cause overflow issues with ELBO-weighted loss at scale.
   265→
   266→5. **GRPO MC sample count:** d1 does not clearly document n_mc (number of Monte Carlo samples for log-probability estimation). The paper's Algorithm 2 references it but the config files only show K=6 rollouts.
   267→
   268→---
   269→
   270→## Sources
   271→
   272→- [LLaDA: Large Language Diffusion Models (arXiv 2502.09992)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.09992)
   273→- [MDLM: Simple and Effective Masked Diffusion Language Models (arXiv 2406.07524)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07524)
   274→- [MDLM GitHub Repository](https://github.com/kuleshov-group/mdlm)
   275→- [Dream 7B: Diffusion Large Language Models (arXiv 2508.15487)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.15487)
   276→- [Dream GitHub Repository](https://github.com/DreamLM/Dream)
   277→- [d1: Scaling Reasoning in Diffusion LLMs via RL (arXiv 2504.12216)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.12216)
   278→- [d1/diffu-GRPO GitHub Repository](https://github.com/dllm-reasoning/d1)
   279→- [Fast-dLLM v2: Efficient Block-Diffusion LLM (arXiv 2509.26328)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.26328)
   280→- [Fast-dLLM GitHub Repository](https://github.com/NVlabs/Fast-dLLM)
   281→- [Mercury: Ultra-Fast Language Models Based on Diffusion (arXiv 2506.17298)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.17298)
   282→- [BD3LM: Block Diffusion (arXiv 2503.09573)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.09573)
   283→- [BD3LM GitHub Repository](https://github.com/kuleshov-group/bd3lms)
   284→- [bitsandbytes 8-bit Optimizers](https://huggingface.co/docs/bitsandbytes/main/en/optimizers)
